proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a new, publicly available radiative transfer tool specifically for galaxy spectra, providing a physical basis for translating observed line fluxes into intrinsic properties. It will quantify the impact of ISM clumpiness and radiative trapping on standard diagnostics, directly improving the calibration of star formation rates and gas masses derived from dust continuum and line emission in the ALMA/JWST era.
proposals.novelty: While line-by-line codes are standard in atmospheric science, their application to the chaotic, multi-phase ISM of entire galaxies is novel. This approach moves beyond the escape probability and large velocity gradient approximations common in astrophysics, offering a more fundamental treatment of photon transport in complex geometries.

proposals.title: A Template Library for High-Redshift Galaxies: Calibrating Spectral Energy Distributions and Dust Corrections from Resolved Local Analogs
proposals.motivation: Interpreting the integrated light of high-redshift galaxies is challenging due to the complex interplay of star formation, dust attenuation, and metallicity, which are often degenerate in unresolved spectra. Pre-2026, large surveys like ZFOURGE have characterized galaxy populations at 1<z<4 using composite SEDs, but these rely on assumptions about the underlying stellar populations and dust geometry. A library of spatially resolved, multi-wavelength observations of nearby galaxies can break these degeneracies by providing empirical templates where the physical conditions are directly measurable.
proposals.hypothesis: That a comprehensive library of resolved spectral energy distributions (SEDs) from nearby galaxies—spanning a range of star formation rates, metallicities, and dust geometries—can serve as accurate templates to decompose and interpret the integrated SEDs of high-redshift galaxies, leading to more robust measurements of their star formation histories and gas masses.
proposals.method: We will construct the template library by leveraging existing multi-wavelength datasets (UV/optical from HST, infrared from Spitzer/Herschel, and radio for H I) for a well-defined sample of nearby galaxies, including those with kinematically anomalous gas like those studied in DIISC-I. For each galaxy, we will extract SEDs from individual star-forming regions and integrated galaxy light. We will use SED fitting codes (e.g., CIGALE, MAGPHYS) to derive physical parameters (star formation rate, dust mass, attenuation curves) for these resolved templates. These templates will then be used in a Bayesian spectral decomposition framework to analyze unresolved high-z galaxy SEDs from surveys like ZFOURGE, using the dust continuum as a gas mass tracer as calibrated in local studies.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce a publicly available, empirically calibrated template library linking resolved local galaxy physics to integrated light properties. It will deliver improved, degeneracy-broken estimates of star formation rates and dust-corrected gas masses for high-redshift galaxies, directly impacting our understanding of galaxy evolution across cosmic time. The method will also provide a new tool to identify potential high-z analogs to local peculiar systems, such as galaxies with anomalous gas kinematics.
proposals.novelty: While SED modeling is common, this approach uniquely uses the full, spatially resolved information from a diverse local galaxy sample—including extreme environments—to create templates, moving beyond simple stellar population models or averaged high-z composites. It directly connects the pre-2026 expertise in resolved local galaxy studies (HI kinematics, dust emission) to the challenge of interpreting unresolved high-z observations.

proposals.title: Dust-Corrected Star Formation Histories as Probes of Systematic Biases in Type Ia Supernova Progenitor Environments
proposals.motivation: The standardization of Type Ia supernovae (SNe Ia) as cosmological distance indicators assumes minimal intrinsic scatter and evolution in their peak luminosities. However, pre-2026 studies suggest progenitor metallicity and age may influence the outcome. The host galaxy's star formation history (SFH), particularly the recent star formation, is a key tracer of these progenitor conditions, but accurate SFHs require robust correction for dust attenuation, a known major uncertainty.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a significant fraction of the observed scatter in standardized SNe Ia luminosities correlates with the dust-corrected, specific star formation rate (sSFR) of their local host environments, and that this correlation evolves with redshift due to changes in average stellar population properties.
proposals.method: We will construct a sample of SNe Ia hosts from surveys like ZFOURGE and the DEEP fields. For each host, we will derive high-fidelity, dust-corrected SFHs by fitting composite spectral energy distributions (SEDs) from UV to far-infrared (including Herschel/PACS/SPIRE data where available) using Bayesian SED-fitting codes (e.g., Prospector, CIGALE), explicitly treating dust emission as a tracer of obscured star formation and gas mass. We will measure the sSFR in the resolved region of the SN explosion (using HST/WFC3 imaging) and correlate this with the SN's Hubble residual. A hierarchical Bayesian model will quantify the intrinsic scatter attributable to local sSFR and its redshift dependence.
proposals.contribution: This work will directly quantify a potential astrophysical systematic for SNe Ia cosmology, moving beyond simple host mass correlations. It will provide an empirical calibration of how progenitor environment influences luminosity, improving the precision of dark energy constraints. Furthermore, it will demonstrate a novel application of panchromatic, dust-aware SED modeling to a critical problem in distance measurement.
proposals.novelty: While host galaxy studies exist, this proposal uniquely integrates Kennicutt's expertise in dust-as-tracer SED fitting and resolved star formation to dissect the SN environment, moving from global host properties to a physically-grounded, local progenitor condition metric derived from a consistent dust-corrected analysis.

proposals.title: A Local Void Distance Ladder: Measuring H0 in Underdense Environments to Test Cosmological Isotropy
proposals.motivation: The persistent tension between local (e.g., SH0ES) and early-universe (e.g., Planck) measurements of the Hubble constant (H0) suggests potential new physics or unaccounted-for systematic errors. Pre-2026 work has largely assumed the local universe is homogeneous on the scales probed by the distance ladder, but theoretical models suggest H0 could vary with local gravitational potential. Testing this requires a direct, precision measurement of H0 in a distinctly different large-scale environment.
proposals.hypothesis: The local value of the Hubble constant, as measured via the classical distance ladder, is independent of large-scale environment; therefore, measurements within cosmic voids will yield a value consistent with those from denser regions like the Local Sheet.
proposals.method: We will construct a dedicated distance ladder within nearby (z < 0.05) cosmic voids identified from surveys like 2MRS or ALFALFA. Targeting void galaxies, we will measure precise distances using the Tip of the Red Giant Branch (TRGB) method with HST for the first rung. For the second rung, we will calibrate mid-infrared Cepheid Period-Luminosity relations in void-anchored galaxies using JWST NIRCam observations, minimizing dust extinction uncertainties. Finally, we will measure Type Ia supernova (SN Ia) luminosities in void-host galaxies to extend the ladder to the Hubble flow. The analysis will leverage Kennicutt's expertise in composite SEDs and dust emission to correct for residual dust and stellar population effects on the calibrators.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first direct, precision constraint on H0 from an underdense environment, offering a critical empirical test for environmental dependence. It will either identify a new systematic tied to large-scale structure or strengthen the case for new physics by showing the tension persists even in voids. The sample of void galaxies with TRGB, Cepheid, and SN Ia data will also be a unique legacy resource for studying star formation and ISM properties in extreme low-density environments.
proposals.novelty: While the distance ladder methodology is established, its application is confined to typical or dense environments. Systematically applying it within the distinct gravitational potential of cosmic voids, using JWST for robust Cepheid photometry in these often low-mass, metal-poor galaxies, is a novel and previously unexplored test of cosmological isotropy.

proposals.title: Calibrating the Local Cepheid Period-Luminosity Relation with High-Redshift Type Ia Supernovae and a Cosmological Prior
proposals.motivation: The calibration of the Cepheid period-luminosity (P-L) relation is the dominant source of systematic uncertainty in the local determination of the Hubble constant (H0). This tension with H0 values derived from the early universe (e.g., Planck CMB) is a major unresolved problem in cosmology. An independent, geometric calibration of local Cepheids, bypassing traditional distance ladder rungs, is critically needed to scrutinize this tension.
proposals.hypothesis: The well-constrained luminosity of high-redshift Type Ia supernovae (SNe Ia), anchored by a flat ΛCDM cosmological model from Planck, can be used to inversely calibrate the absolute magnitude of Cepheid variables in nearby SN Ia host galaxies, providing a novel, cosmology-constrained zero-point for the Cepheid P-L relation.
proposals.method: We will construct a sample of nearby galaxies (z < 0.01) hosting both Cepheids (from HST and JWST) and well-observed SNe Ia. For each galaxy, we will use the observed SN Ia peak magnitude and a redshift-based distance modulus derived from the Planck ΛCDM cosmology (H0=67.4 km/s/Mpc, Ωm=0.315) to infer the galaxy's absolute distance. This distance will then calibrate the mean absolute magnitude of its Cepheid population. We will perform a hierarchical Bayesian fit to determine the zero-point and metallicity dependence of the P-L relation from this ensemble, carefully propagating uncertainties from dust extinction (using infrared HST/WFC3 and JWST/NIRCam photometry) and SN Ia light curve parameters.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce a Cepheid P-L relation calibrated directly by early-universe cosmology, offering a powerful cross-check on traditional ladder calibrations. It will quantify the systematic shift in H0 if the Cepheid scale is forced to align with the Planck cosmology, directly testing if local distance ladder systematics can resolve the H0 tension.
proposals.novelty: This inverts the classical distance ladder, using the high-redshift universe to calibrate local standards. While SNe Ia are typically calibrated by Cepheids, we use the cosmological constancy of SNe Ia at high-z to calibrate Cepheids, a method made newly viable by precise CMB cosmology and JWST's ability to characterize Cepheid hosts.

proposals.title: Correcting for Local Bulk Flows: A New Dust-Based Calibration of Star Formation Rates and Gas Masses in the Local Volume
proposals.motivation: Accurate distances and redshifts are fundamental for calibrating key scaling relations in galaxy evolution, such as the Kennicutt-Schmidt law linking star formation rate (SFR) to gas mass. In the Local Volume (<~40 Mpc), the assumption of negligible peculiar velocities is often violated, introducing significant scatter and bias in these calibrations. This directly impacts the foundational SFR and gas mass indicators used for high-redshift studies, including those derived from infrared and dust continuum emission.
proposals.hypothesis: By constructing a refined model of the local velocity field and applying it to correct galaxy redshifts, we can significantly reduce the scatter in the calibration of dust-based SFR and gas mass tracers, leading to more precise and accurate benchmarks for high-redshift galaxy populations.
proposals.method: We will construct a sample of ~500 nearby galaxies (D < 40 Mpc) with high-quality distance measurements (e.g., Cepheids, TRGB, SBF) and multi-wavelength photometry from GALEX, WISE, and Herschel. We will model the local bulk flow using a multi-attractor model (e.g., Virgo, Great Attractor) to derive peculiar velocity corrections. For each galaxy, we will compute corrected SFRs (from UV+IR SEDs) and total gas masses (from dust continuum scaling relations, e.g., Scoville et al. 2016). We will then compare the scatter and slope of the SFR-gas relation before and after the velocity correction.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a crucial, velocity-corrected calibration of the SFR-gas relation in the local universe, directly improving the accuracy of dust-based SFR and gas mass measurements used in surveys like ZFOURGE. It will also deliver a publicly available catalog of corrected distances and physical parameters for a key local benchmark sample.
proposals.novelty: While bulk flows are studied for cosmology, their systematic impact on the calibration of fundamental galaxy scaling relations—specifically those using dust emission as a tracer—has not been comprehensively addressed. This proposal uniquely integrates velocity field modeling with panchromatic SED analysis to refine the local anchors for high-z galaxy evolution studies.

proposals.title: Calibrating the Cepheid Period-Luminosity Relation with the Geometric Distance to NGC 4258: A Direct Test of Stellar Physics and Its Impact on H0
proposals.motivation: The persistent tension between local and early-universe measurements of the Hubble constant (H0) suggests potential systematic errors in the cosmic distance ladder. A key rung is the Cepheid Period-Luminosity (PL) relation, traditionally calibrated in the Large Magellanic Cloud, which may harbor metallicity effects or other astrophysical systematics. Directly calibrating the Cepheid PL relation using a geometric distance within a single galaxy offers a critical, independent check on these stellar physics, directly impacting the accuracy of H0.
proposals.hypothesis: The Cepheid PL relation, when calibrated directly via the precise geometric distance to NGC 4258 from water maser observations, will yield a zero-point and metallicity dependence consistent with, or revealing systematic deviations from, the canonical LMC-based calibration, thereby refining the local H0 measurement.
proposals.method: We will assemble a multi-wavelength dataset for Cepheids in NGC 4258, leveraging archival HST optical (e.g., F555W, F814W) and new near-infrared (e.g., F160W) photometry to construct Wesenheit magnitudes that minimize dust extinction effects. We will apply the geometric distance to NGC 4258 (7.60 ± 0.17 Mpc) to calibrate the PL relation zero-point directly. This will be combined with a sample of Cepheids in other galaxies from the SH0ES program, using the calibrated relation to measure distances and ultimately H0. The analysis will explicitly test for a metallicity term by incorporating spectroscopic metallicities for Cepheid hosts.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the most direct geometric calibration of the Cepheid PL relation to date, isolating and quantifying potential systematics in the primary standard candle. It will produce an independent, robust local H0 value, critically informing the H0 tension debate. Furthermore, it will deliver an empirically grounded metallicity correction for Cepheids, essential for high-precision extragalactic distance measurements.
proposals.novelty: While NGC 4258's distance is used in distance-ladder analyses, this proposal focuses on using it as a *direct laboratory* to calibrate fundamental Cepheid physics, decoupling this calibration from the traditional LMC anchor and the associated uncertainties in the LMC distance and metallicity gradient.

proposals.title: Regulation of the Star Formation Threshold by Stellar Feedback: A Multi-Wavelength Study of the Kennicutt-Schmidt Relation in Nearby Galaxies
proposals.motivation: The Kennicutt-Schmidt (K-S) relation posits a critical gas density threshold for star formation, but its physical origin remains debated. Pre-2026 work often treats this threshold as a cause of star formation. However, stellar feedback (radiation pressure, winds, supernovae) is known to disrupt and heat the interstellar medium, potentially setting the observed density threshold as an effect, not a cause.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that stellar feedback actively regulates the gas density in star-forming regions, establishing the observed K-S threshold. The threshold is therefore a consequence of feedback-driven equilibrium, not a prerequisite for star formation.
proposals.method: We will analyze a sample of nearby galaxies (e.g., from DIISC, THINGS) with high-resolution H I and CO maps to trace atomic and molecular gas surface density. We will combine this with Hα and Spitzer/Herschel infrared data to map star formation rate surface density and dust-obscured star formation. Using pixel-by-pixel analysis, we will measure the gas density at which the K-S relation steepens and correlate this local threshold with local feedback tracers (e.g., stellar population age from SED fitting, dust temperature, non-thermal radio emission) to test for causal links.
proposals.contribution: This work will directly test a feedback-regulated origin for the star formation threshold, moving beyond descriptive correlations to a dynamical model. It will provide a physical framework for interpreting high-redshift galaxy surveys (like ZFOURGE) where gas properties are inferred from dust continuum emission.
proposals.novelty: This inverts the classical causal interpretation of the K-S threshold. Instead of asking what gas density is needed for star formation, we ask if star formation itself determines what gas densities are possible, a non-obvious shift in perspective given the established paradigm.

proposals.title: A Bayesian Hierarchical Model for the Correlation Between Dust-Obscured Star Formation and Galaxy Morphology in the Local Universe
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026 studies, including Kennicutt's work on dust as a gas mass tracer, show that dust emission and star formation are linked, but the strength and nature of this correlation across different galaxy types (e.g., spirals, irregulars, mergers) remains poorly quantified. Robustly quantifying these weak or type-dependent correlations is essential for accurately calibrating star formation rate (SFR) indicators and understanding how the interstellar medium regulates star formation in diverse galactic environments.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the correlation between far-infrared dust continuum emission (a tracer of gas mass and SFR) and other galactic properties (e.g., Hα emission, stellar mass) is not universal but varies systematically as a function of galaxy morphology and local environment, with these variations being revealed through a unified Bayesian framework.
proposals.method: We will apply Bayesian hierarchical modeling to a multi-wavelength sample of local galaxies, combining archival infrared (e.g., Herschel, Spitzer) and optical (e.g., Hα) data. The model will treat the parameters of the dust-SFR correlation (slope, intercept, intrinsic scatter) as themselves being drawn from population-level distributions conditioned on galaxy type (from ZFOURGE-like SED fitting) and environment. We will use Hamiltonian Monte Carlo sampling via Stan to perform inference, robustly propagating measurement uncertainties and upper limits while quantifying the posterior probability of different correlation strengths for each galaxy class.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first statistically rigorous, probabilistic calibration of dust-based star formation and gas mass tracers across the galaxy population. It will deliver posterior distributions for correlation parameters, offering a more nuanced tool for interpreting observations of high-redshift galaxies where morphology is unresolved. The analysis will directly inform the physical interpretation of kinematically anomalous gas clouds, like those found in DIISC-I, by contextualizing their dust properties.
proposals.novelty: While linear fits to galaxy scaling relations are common, applying a full Bayesian hierarchical model to explicitly quantify how the *parameters* of the dust-SFR relation depend on galaxy type is novel. This moves beyond binning data and fitting separate regressions to a unified model that learns the population structure of the correlation directly from the data.

proposals.title: Environmental Quenching and Enrichment: A Multi-Tracer Gradient Analysis of Gas, Dust, and Metallicity in the Virgo Cluster
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026 studies of galaxy evolution have established the profound influence of cluster environments, driving processes like ram-pressure stripping and strangulation that quench star formation. However, a unified, spatially resolved view of how these mechanisms alter the internal chemical evolution and gas/dust budgets of galaxies across a full cluster population is lacking. This limits our understanding of the feedback loop between environment, gas removal, and metal enrichment.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the radial gradients of gas-phase metallicity, dust-to-gas ratio, and specific star formation rate within cluster galaxies will show systematic trends with cluster-centric radius and galaxy morphology, directly tracing the efficiency and timescales of environmental quenching and enrichment processes.
proposals.method: We will apply a consistent gradient analysis to a statistically complete sample of star-forming galaxies in the nearby Virgo Cluster. Using archival and new multi-wavelength data, we will construct resolved maps of key tracers: optical emission lines (for metallicity via strong-line calibrations and star formation rate), Herschel/Spitzer infrared fluxes (for dust mass), and HI/CO data (for atomic and molecular gas). Gradients will be measured by performing azimuthally averaged radial profile fitting on these maps. We will then correlate the gradient slopes and normalizations with each galaxy's cluster-centric distance, stellar mass, and morphological class.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first comprehensive, multi-tracer quantification of how cluster environments reshape galaxies from the inside out. It will directly link observable abundance gradients to physical quenching mechanisms, offering empirical constraints for simulations of environmental effects. The analysis will also test the robustness of dust continuum emission as a tracer of gas mass under varying environmental conditions.
proposals.novelty: While gradient studies exist for individual galaxies or specific tracers, applying a systematic, multi-parameter gradient analysis across a full cluster galaxy population to disentangle environmental effects is novel. It synergistically combines Kennicutt's expertise in star formation, dust, and gas tracers with the spatial analysis of environmental impact.

proposals.title: Reconstructing the Radial Star Formation History of M101: An Inversion of Abundance Gradients and Gas Tracers
proposals.motivation: The radial abundance gradients in disk galaxies are a fossil record of their inside-out growth, but directly inverting these gradients to constrain the detailed radial star formation history (rSFH) remains a significant challenge. M101, with its well-measured steep metallicity gradient and extensive multi-wavelength data, serves as an ideal test case. This work will bridge the gap between observed present-day chemical patterns and the physical processes of gas accretion, enrichment, and radial migration that shaped the disk.
proposals.hypothesis: The present-day radial gradients of gas-phase and stellar abundances in M101 can be inverted, using a physically motivated chemical evolution model, to uniquely constrain the time-resolved star formation history as a function of galactocentric radius.
proposals.method: We will combine deep, spatially resolved optical spectroscopy (for O/H and N/O gradients) with archival infrared and UV data to map current star formation and dust-corrected stellar mass. A Bayesian Markov Chain Monte Carlo (MCMC) framework will be employed to invert a flexible chemical evolution model, treating the rSFH in radial annuli as a set of coupled time-dependent functions. The model will incorporate gas infall prescriptions and be constrained by the observed abundance gradients, gas-to-dust mass ratios (from DUST CONTINUUM EMISSION AS A TRACER OF GAS MASS), and the present-day stellar mass profile.
proposals.contribution: This study will produce the first quantitatively derived, time-resolved radial SFH for a nearby grand-design spiral, moving beyond parametric descriptions. It will directly test predictions of inside-out formation models and quantify the role of radial gas flows. The developed inversion framework will be a critical tool for interpreting high-redshift galaxy data from upcoming observatories.
proposals.novelty: While gradients are commonly used to *test* SFH models, the direct, non-parametric inversion to recover the rSFH itself is a novel, computationally intensive inverse problem. The integration of dust continuum-derived gas masses as a key model constraint, leveraging Kennicutt's recent work, provides a crucial physical anchor distinct from purely chemical approaches.

proposals.title: Calibrating Stellar Nucleosynthesis and Feedback Models with Nebular Abundances in Local Star-Forming Galaxies
proposals.motivation: Stellar population synthesis and chemical evolution models rely on theoretical nucleosynthesis yields, which have significant uncertainties, especially for massive stars and asymptotic giant branch stars. These uncertainties propagate into predictions for galaxy spectra, gas-phase metallicities, and dust production. Robust, empirically derived nebular abundances from H II regions in nearby galaxies provide a critical, independent benchmark that is currently underutilized for calibrating these fundamental stellar inputs.
proposals.hypothesis: By using spatially resolved, robust nebular abundance measurements (e.g., O, N, Ne) as fixed constraints, we can empirically calibrate the net effective yields and mass-loss prescriptions in stellar evolution models, leading to more accurate predictions for integrated galaxy spectral energy distributions and dust-to-gas ratios.
proposals.method: We will construct a sample of nearby, well-resolved star-forming galaxies (e.g., from the DUST continuum and DIISC projects) with deep optical spectroscopy for nebular abundance mapping and matched infrared photometry. For each galaxy, we will use the ZFOURGE SED-fitting methodology to derive stellar population parameters, but will invert the standard process. We will fix the final metallicity in the models to the observed nebular abundance in concentric annuli and iteratively adjust the nucleosynthesis yield tables and stellar feedback prescriptions in the underlying stellar evolution tracks (e.g., MIST, Geneva) until the modeled stellar continuum and dust properties (from IR) consistently reproduce the observed multi-wavelength data.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce a set of empirically calibrated yield prescriptions for use in galaxy evolution models. It will directly reduce a key systematic uncertainty in interpreting high-redshift galaxy spectra and in linking stellar mass growth to chemical enrichment. The results will also provide new insights into the origin of observed variations in mass-metallicity relations and dust-to-metal ratios.
proposals.novelty: While nebular abundances are commonly used to *test* models, systematically inverting the problem to *calibrate* the fundamental stellar astrophysics inputs within a panchromatic, spatially resolved framework is a novel approach. This leverages Kennicutt's expertise in combining IR dust tracers with star formation diagnostics to break degeneracies in the modeling.

proposals.title: Calibrating the Dust-to-Gas Ratio and Star Formation Law at Cosmic Noon: A Multi-Ion Temperature Analysis of JWST NIRSpec Analogue Galaxies
proposals.motivation: Understanding the physics of star formation in galaxies at the peak epoch of cosmic star formation (z~1-3) is a primary goal of extragalactic astronomy. A key uncertainty is the calibration of the dust-to-gas ratio and the Kennicutt-Schmidt law at high redshift, where metallicities and interstellar medium conditions differ from the local universe. The James Webb Space Telescope (JWST) now provides rest-frame optical spectra for high-redshift galaxies, but robust gas mass estimates, crucial for these calibrations, remain challenging.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the multi-ion temperature method, applied to rest-frame optical far-infrared fine-structure lines observed by JWST/NIRSpec in low-redshift analogues, can accurately calibrate the dust-to-gas ratio. This calibrated ratio can then be used to derive gas masses from dust continuum in high-redshift galaxies, enabling a more precise measurement of the star formation law at cosmic noon.
proposals.method: We will select a sample of low-redshift (z<0.1) 'analogue' galaxies from archival surveys (e.g., KINGFISH, GOALS) with rich multi-wavelength data, including Herschel/PACS/SPIRE far-infrared fine-structure lines ([CII]158μm, [OI]63μm, [OIII]88μm) and ALMA dust continuum maps. For these analogues, we will apply the multi-ion temperature method, using the ratios of these lines to constrain the gas temperature and density, and thereby derive accurate gas masses independent of dust assumptions. We will then correlate these gas masses with ALMA-derived dust masses to establish a locally calibrated dust-to-gas ratio as a function of metallicity and other galaxy properties. Finally, we will apply this calibrated relation to a sample of 1<z<4 galaxies observed with JWST/NIRSpec (for metallicity) and ALMA (for dust continuum) from programs like ZFOURGE and its follow-ups.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel, empirically calibrated dust-to-gas ratio for high-redshift galaxies, significantly reducing systematic uncertainties in gas mass estimates derived from dust continuum. It will yield the most robust measurement to date of the Kennicutt-Schmidt star formation law at cosmic noon, testing its universality and dependence on galactic environment. The results will directly inform models of galaxy evolution during a critical epoch.
proposals.novelty: While the multi-ion temperature method is established for local galaxies, its application to calibrate the high-redshift dust-to-gas ratio via JWST-observed analogues is novel. This bridges the gap between direct gas tracers available locally and the dust-based proxies necessary at high redshift, leveraging JWST's unique spectroscopic capabilities for the first time in this context.

proposals.title: Disruption of Chemical Evolution in Interacting Galaxies: A Direct-Method Census of H II Region Abundance Gradients
proposals.motivation: Galaxy interactions are a primary driver of morphological transformation and star formation enhancement, yet their impact on the internal chemical evolution of galaxies remains poorly quantified. Pre-2026 studies of abundance gradients in isolated galaxies provide a baseline, but systematic, spatially resolved metallicity measurements across a statistical sample of interacting pairs are lacking. This gap limits our understanding of how gas flows, triggered by tidal forces, redistribute metals and disrupt the secular processes that establish radial abundance gradients.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that strong gravitational interactions disrupt the typically monotonic radial metallicity gradients of disk galaxies, leading to flattened or inverted gradients and increased azimuthal scatter in abundances, with the degree of disruption correlating with the interaction stage and strength.
proposals.method: We will apply the 'direct' Te-method to measure oxygen abundances in a statistically significant sample of H II regions (N > 500) across ~20 nearby (z < 0.03) interacting galaxy pairs. Regions will be identified using a multi-wavelength approach, combining H-alpha (from surveys like PHANGS-MUSE) and mid-infrared (from WISE) to correct for dust extinction. We will utilize existing optical integral field spectroscopy (e.g., from MaNGA, archival MUSE) and new targeted spectroscopic follow-up to obtain [O III] λ4363 detections. Gradient analysis will employ robust Bayesian linear regression to fit radial trends and quantify intrinsic scatter.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce the first large-scale, direct-method map of chemical disruption in interacting galaxies, quantifying the relationship between interaction parameters and gradient breakdown. It will provide crucial empirical constraints for simulations of galaxy evolution and feedback, and establish a local benchmark for interpreting chemical signatures in high-redshift merging systems observed with JWST.
proposals.novelty: While gradients have been studied in isolated galaxies, applying the rigorous direct method systematically across the diverse environments of interacting pairs—linking gradient morphology to specific tidal features and star-forming regions—is a novel, observationally demanding approach that moves beyond single-object studies or strong-line method calibrations.

proposals.title: Constraining the Integrated Stellar IMF in Nearby Star-Forming Galaxies via Chemo-Spectral Population Synthesis
proposals.motivation: The stellar initial mass function (IMF) is a cornerstone of galaxy evolution models, yet its universality remains debated. Pre-2026 studies, including those using dust-corrected emission lines, reveal systematically lower central oxygen abundances in star-forming galaxies than predicted by standard calibrations. This discrepancy suggests a potential bias in abundance measurements tied to the underlying stellar populations and their IMF.
proposals.hypothesis: The observed depression in central gas-phase abundances, after careful correction for dust attenuation and excitation, can be explained by variations in the integrated galactic IMF, specifically an excess of massive stars enriching the ISM, rather than a true metallicity deficit.
proposals.method: We will construct a sample of nearby star-forming galaxies with high-quality, dust-corrected optical emission-line maps (e.g., from MUSE) and matched infrared photometry (from WISE/Spitzer) to constrain the stellar and nebular components. Using a Bayesian framework, we will perform chemo-spectral population synthesis (building on ZFOURGE SED methods) with the stellar IMF as a free parameter. The model will simultaneously fit the panchromatic SED and the spatially resolved, corrected abundance patterns, iteratively solving the inverse problem to derive the IMF shape most consistent with the data.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel, empirically derived constraint on the integrated galactic IMF in the local universe, independent of dynamical methods. It will establish a direct link between chemical abundance patterns and stellar population properties, refining our interpretation of galaxy spectra and the baryon cycle.
proposals.novelty: While IMF studies often rely on stellar dynamics or UV/IR spectral features, this approach inverts the problem by using the dust-corrected nebular abundance pattern—a direct product of past stellar populations—as the primary constraint, integrating Kennicutt's expertise in star formation tracers and dust-corrected emission.

proposals.title: Quantifying the Diffuse Ionized Gas Contribution to H II Region Emission: A Multi-Wavelength Decomposition Study
proposals.motivation: H II regions are fundamental probes of massive star formation and chemical enrichment in galaxies. However, pre-2026 analyses often treat them as isolated systems, subtracting a local background that may not fully account for the pervasive, warm diffuse ionized gas (DIG) permeating galaxy disks. This assumption can systematically bias key derived properties like star formation rates, ionization parameters, and chemical abundances, compromising our understanding of stellar feedback and baryon cycling.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a significant and spatially variable fraction of the emission traditionally attributed to H II regions originates from the surrounding diffuse interstellar medium, and that properly isolating this component will reveal systematic trends in H II region properties with galactic environment.
proposals.method: We will use high-resolution narrowband H-alpha and [S II] imagery from telescopes like HST and VLT/MUSE for a sample of nearby galaxies (e.g., from the DUST continuum studies), combined with Spitzer and Herschel infrared maps to trace dust-obscured emission. For each H II region identified via automated algorithms (e.g., HIIphot, adapted to our data), we will perform a multi-component spectral energy distribution (SED) fitting, informed by the ZFOURGE composite SED techniques, to decompose the emission into a compact H II region and a diffuse DIG component. The [S II]/H-alpha ratio will be used as a key diagnostic to constrain the DIG contribution.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first systematic, quantitative correction for DIG contamination in large H II region samples, leading to more accurate calibrations of star formation rate indicators and metallicity diagnostics. It will directly test models of photon leakage and the ionization budget of galaxies, linking the physics of individual star-forming regions to the global interstellar medium.
proposals.novelty: While the existence of the DIG is known, its quantitative impact on the measured properties of *individual* H II regions across diverse galactic environments has not been rigorously assessed. This proposal moves beyond simple background subtraction to a physically motivated decomposition, leveraging multi-wavelength data and SED-fitting techniques previously applied to whole galaxies.

proposals.title: The Aromatic Feature-Ionization Index Correlation: A Universal Calibration Across Diverse Galaxy Populations
proposals.motivation: The aromatic (PAH) feature-to-continuum ratio and the ionization index (e.g., [Ne III]/[Ne II]) are powerful, dust-insensitive diagnostics of star formation conditions. A correlation between them has been established in nearby star-forming regions and a limited set of galaxies, suggesting a link between massive stellar populations and PAH excitation. However, its universality and physical drivers remain untested across the full spectrum of galaxy properties, including metallicity, star formation rate, and dominant ionization source (AGN vs. star formation).
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the correlation between the aromatic feature strength and the mid-infrared ionization index is a fundamental, universal relationship for star-forming galaxies, but that its slope and scatter are systematically modulated by global galaxy properties such as metallicity and specific star formation rate.
proposals.method: We will assemble a large, diverse sample of ~500 galaxies from archival Spitzer/IRS spectra, supplemented by new James Webb Space Telescope/MIRI data for high-z targets, ensuring coverage from metal-poor dwarfs to luminous infrared galaxies. We will uniformly measure the 6.2/7.7 μm PAH equivalent widths and the [Ne III] 15.6μm / [Ne II] 12.8μm line ratio. Composite SED fitting (as in ZFOURGE) will derive stellar masses and dust-corrected SFRs, while ancillary optical/NIR spectra will provide metallicities. We will perform multi-variate regression analysis to quantify the correlation's dependence on these secondary parameters.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a robust, calibrated diagnostic for inferring the hardness of the interstellar radiation field from PAH emission alone, applicable to dusty galaxies where optical diagnostics fail. It will critically inform the use of PAH features as tracers of star formation and chemical evolution in upcoming JWST surveys, and provide empirical constraints on PAH destruction and excitation models.
proposals.novelty: While previous studies noted the correlation in homogenous samples, this is the first systematic investigation to test its invariance across the full parameter space of galaxy mass, metallicity, and redshift, explicitly linking integrated galaxy properties to the physics of PAH emission.

proposals.title: DUSTPOP: A Population Synthesis Framework for Mixed Dust Grain Compositions and Their Panchromatic Emission
proposals.motivation: Interstellar dust is a critical, multi-phase component governing galaxy evolution, from obscuring star formation to catalyzing molecular gas formation. Current spectral energy distribution (SED) modeling, as used in projects like ZFOURGE, often treats dust as a simple, homogeneous screen or uses fixed grain property distributions, limiting the physical interpretation of infrared and submillimeter data. A more sophisticated treatment is needed to link observed dust continuum emission, a key tracer of gas mass, to the true physical conditions and evolutionary stage of galaxies, especially at high redshift.
proposals.hypothesis: By adapting the formalism of stellar population synthesis to dust grains, we hypothesize that the panchromatic emission from a galaxy can be decomposed into distinct 'dust populations' characterized by grain composition, size distribution, and heating mechanism, thereby providing more accurate estimates of dust-obscured star formation rates and interstellar medium properties.
proposals.method: We will develop a modular dust population synthesis (DPS) code. The core will be a grid of single-grain-type emission models (e.g., silicate, graphite, polycyclic aromatic hydrocarbons) using pre-2026 optical constants and Mie theory. We will implement a non-negative matrix factorization (NMF) algorithm to fit observed galaxy SEDs (from UV to sub-mm) with linear combinations of these basis 'dust populations,' analogous to stellar population fitting. The method will be tested and calibrated using a sample of local galaxies with well-characterized dust properties from Herschel and Spitzer data, and then applied to high-redshift samples from surveys like ZFOURGE.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce a publicly available software tool for decomposing dust emission, moving beyond a simple 'dust mass' or 'temperature' to a more physical characterization of grain populations. It will directly improve the accuracy of using dust continuum as a gas mass tracer by accounting for grain property variations. The framework will also provide new diagnostics to identify galaxies with anomalous dust properties, such as those hinted at in studies of kinematically anomalous HI clouds.
proposals.novelty: While stellar population synthesis is mature, its formal adaptation to model dust as distinct, co-evolving populations with quantifiable contributions to the total SED is novel. This approach moves beyond fixed dust models to a flexible, empirical decomposition that can reveal variations in grain chemistry and size distribution across different galactic environments.

proposals.title: The Metallicity Dependence of the Cepheid Period-Luminosity Relation in the Near-Infrared: A Calibration for Dust-Obscured Star-Forming Regions
proposals.motivation: The Cepheid period-luminosity (P-L) relation is the cornerstone of the extragalactic distance ladder, but its sensitivity to metallicity remains a dominant systematic uncertainty in measuring the Hubble constant. Pre-2026 studies have primarily focused on optical bands, where the metallicity effect and dust extinction are both significant and difficult to disentangle. Near-infrared (NIR) J, H, and K bands are less affected by extinction and may offer a cleaner probe of the intrinsic stellar physics, yet a precise, empirical calibration of the metallicity dependence in these bands is lacking.
proposals.hypothesis: The slope of the Cepheid P-L relation in the NIR (J, H, K bands) has a measurable, but significantly reduced, dependence on host galaxy metallicity compared to the optical relation. This dependence can be characterized and corrected using integrated metallicities from H II regions in the host galaxies.
proposals.method: We will construct a sample of Cepheid-rich galaxies spanning a range of metallicities, leveraging archival HST optical data for Cepheid discovery and period measurement. For each host galaxy, we will use ground-based NIR imaging (e.g., from UKIRT or VISTA) to measure mean J, H, and K magnitudes for the Cepheids. Galaxy metallicities will be derived using the strong-line method applied to integrated spectra of H II regions from surveys like CHAOS or from our own follow-up, tying into Kennicutt's expertise in star-forming region diagnostics. We will perform multi-linear regression to simultaneously fit the P-L relation and its metallicity term (Δlog P/Δ[O/H]) in each NIR band.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first multi-band NIR empirical calibration of the Cepheid metallicity effect, directly reducing a key systematic in distance measurements. It will inform the optimal choice of NIR band for future distance ladder campaigns in dusty, metal-rich, or metal-poor environments. The results will be critical for next-generation telescopes like JWST and the ELT, which will observe Cepheids primarily in the infrared.
proposals.novelty: While the metallicity effect is known in principle, this study uniquely focuses on a homogeneous, empirical calibration across the J, H, and K bands by directly linking Cepheid photometry to host galaxy metallicities derived from H II regions, a method aligned with the PI's recent work on gas and dust in galaxies.

ideas.title: Quantifying Gravitational Perturbation Efficiency in Triggering Star Formation via a Reaction-Diffusion Framework
ideas.description: This research adapts a solution from epidemiology to quantify the role of gravitational interactions in triggering extended star formation. In epidemiology, reaction-diffusion models and the basic reproduction number (R0) are used to separate and quantify the efficiency of a triggering event (an infection) from background rates. We propose to model the NGC 4618 and NGC 4625A system using an analogous astrophysical reaction-diffusion framework. The 'reaction' component is the local Kennicutt-Schmidt star formation law, describing the intrinsic conversion of gas to stars. The 'diffusion' component models the propagation of gravitational perturbation energy (density waves, tidal shocks) from the interacting companion through the galactic disk. By fitting this model to multi-wavelength observations (HI, Hα, stellar continuum), we will calculate a dimensionless 'triggering efficiency parameter' (Τ). This parameter quantifies the excess, extended star formation directly attributable to the perturbative diffusion from NGC 4625A, providing a rigorous, transferable metric for the interaction's causative role, much as R0 does for an epidemic's origin.

ideas.title: Extend analysis to a larger sample of galaxies

ideas.title: Calibrate the Low-Mass IMF via Dynamical Masses in Inclination-Corrected UV Complexes
ideas.description: This project targets isolated, UV-bright stellar complexes to measure their stellar populations and IMF. By obtaining precise inclination data for these same systems, we can correct their emission linewidths (e.g., H-alpha) to derive accurate gas dynamical masses. The synergy lies in directly comparing the stellar mass—inferred from the population synthesis and a *hypothesized* IMF—with the independent dynamical total mass. A significant discrepancy, particularly a 'mass deficit' in the stellar budget, would provide direct empirical evidence for a bottom-heavy or top-heavy IMF in these unique, low-density environments, moving IMF studies beyond local calibrations.

ideas.title: Scale up to high-redshift galaxy analogs

ideas.title: Bayesian probabilistic inclination modeling for robust dynamical mass comparisons of distant ELGs
ideas.description: This approach develops a Bayesian framework to correct emission linewidths for inclination by treating galaxy orientation as a probabilistic parameter rather than a single measured value. It synthesizes multiple observational proxies for inclination—such as morphological axis ratios from high-resolution imaging, kinematic major axes from integral field spectroscopy, and constraints from velocity dispersion maps—to build a joint likelihood model. The output is a posterior probability distribution for the dynamical mass of each galaxy, which fully incorporates the uncertainty inherent in the inclination correction. This allows for statistically rigorous comparisons of mass distributions between galaxy populations, directly addressing the systematic biases introduced by the complex and irregular structures typical of high-redshift ELGs.

ideas.title: Quantifying the Distinct Chemical Imprint of Mergers on Emission Line Galaxies
ideas.description: This study moves beyond a simple correlation test to isolate the specific chemical signature of galaxy interactions. It employs a tiered analysis of integral field unit (IFU) and deep imaging data for a large sample of ELGs, comparing interacting systems to a carefully matched control sample in mass, redshift, and local density. The research quantifies deviations in metallicity, alpha-enhancement, and their spatial distributions relative to kinematic disturbance indicators and quantitative merger stages. The final output is a empirical model that identifies which chemical properties are uniquely attributable to merger-driven processes versus secular evolution or accretion.

ideas.title: Bayesian Inference on the H I-Poor UV Complex Population: Quantifying the Probability of Truly Hydrogen-Deficient Star Formation
ideas.description: This research program systematically searches for UV-bright star-forming regions lacking H I peaks and employs a hierarchical Bayesian model to analyze them. The model incorporates uncertainties from data reduction, H I column density limits, stellar population synthesis, and the stochastic sampling of the IMF to compute the posterior probability that a region's observed H I level is physically deficient. This allows for a rigorous statistical classification of candidates and the inference of population-level parameters, such as the true incidence rate and environmental dependencies of H I-poor UV complexes, testing fundamental assumptions about gas prerequisites for star formation.

ideas.title: A Tiered, Forward-Modeled Survey for Robust Chemical Evolution at High Redshift
ideas.description: This redesigned approach overcomes the sensitivity barrier of high-redshift spectroscopy by implementing a two-tier observational strategy. The first tier uses deep, high-resolution spectroscopy from 8m+ class telescopes on a pristine, mass-complete sample of z~0.5-1.5 galaxies to establish anchor points. The second tier leverages large, deep, lower-resolution multi-object surveys (e.g., DESI, PFS) to gather chemical information for thousands of galaxies, providing statistical power. A unified Bayesian forward-modeling pipeline is applied to both datasets, explicitly modeling the effects of noise, resolution, and selection to derive robust, population-averaged chemical enrichment histories for earlier cosmic epochs.

ideas.title: Adapting Ecological Population Sampling to Constrain the IMF in UV Complexes
ideas.description: This research adapts the ecological solution of adaptive cluster sampling to determine the stellar initial mass function (IMF) in low-mass, isolated UV-bright complexes. The analogous problem in ecology is efficiently mapping the population structure of a rare, clustered species without exhaustive surveys. The adapted solution involves using integral field unit (IFU) or multi-object spectroscopy on a new generation of telescopes to perform an initial sparse sampling of a UV complex. When a spectrum indicative of recent star formation (tracing high-mass stars) is detected, the observational strategy adapts by adding pointings in the immediate vicinity to fully map that stellar sub-population. This method efficiently constructs a resolved inventory of stellar masses within the complex, mitigating biases from integrated light and providing a robust, spatially-aware constraint on the IMF in these isolated environments.

ideas.title: Adapting Epidemiological Contact Tracing to Map Merger Influence on ELG Chemistry
ideas.description: This idea challenges the assumption that Extreme Emission Line Galaxies (ELGs) are purely isolated star-forming systems by investigating if interactions or mergers influence their observed chemical properties. It does so by drawing an analogy from epidemiology, where the spread of disease variants is understood by tracing contact networks and correlating pathogen markers with interaction history. The adapted solution treats galactic interactions as 'contact events' and uses techniques inspired by epidemiological contact tracing and transmission modeling. By constructing a statistical framework that correlates chemical signatures (e.g., [O/Fe], [N/O], metallicity gradients) with quantitative measures of galactic environment and interaction history (e.g., neighbor density, tidal disturbance indices), the research aims to map the 'transmission' of chemical anomalies, thereby distinguishing between properties forged in isolation and those influenced by mergers.

ideas.title: Apply Seismic Tomography's Velocity-to-Structure Mapping for Kinematic Inclination Correction
ideas.description: This idea adapts the solution from seismic tomography in geophysics to the problem of estimating inclinations for distant Emission Line Galaxies (ELGs). In seismology, an inverse problem is solved using seismic wave velocities from known paths to model the Earth's hidden 3D structure, transferring local correlations to a global model. Analogously, we propose to use kinematic modeling (velocity fields) from a library of nearby galaxies with robust, independently measured inclinations to train a probabilistic inversion model. This model learns the complex mapping between observable kinematic signatures in resolved data and the galaxy's intrinsic inclination. Once trained, this model can be applied as a transfer function to the limited kinematic data (e.g., integrated line width, spatial gradient from slit spectroscopy) of a distant, unresolved ELG to statistically infer its most probable inclination, correcting measurements of its intrinsic properties like mass and star formation rate.

ideas.title: Applying Climate Ensemble Modeling to Galactic Interaction Predictions
ideas.description: This idea adapts the ensemble modeling solution from climate science to the original proposal of using cosmological simulation techniques on the local NGC 4625 system. In climate science, ensemble modeling is used to manage uncertainty by running multiple simulations with varied inputs, leading to more robust, probabilistic forecasts. This approach is adapted by applying it to high-resolution N-body/hydro simulations of the interacting NGC 4625/4618 system. An ensemble of simulations will be run, varying initial orbital parameters, gas fractions, and feedback prescriptions. The output will be analyzed not as a single deterministic history, but as a probabilistic suite of outcomes, allowing astronomers to identify the most robust predicted features (like tidal tails and star formation bursts) and formally quantify the uncertainties in modeling the system's evolution.

ideas.title: Applying Paleoclimatology Ice Core Layering to High-Redshift Galaxy Spectral Stacking
ideas.description: This idea adapts a solution from paleoclimatology, where ice core layers are stacked to reveal ancient climate signals, to the challenge of studying chemical evolution in the early universe. The analogous problem is detecting faint, time-specific signals from distant epochs. The adapted research idea is to apply spectral stacking techniques to large samples of galaxies at z > 0.5. By aligning and co-adding their spectra in rest-frame, the method statistically amplifies weak spectral features diagnostic of chemical composition, thereby 'pushing to higher redshift' to probe chemical evolution at earlier cosmic times with greater precision than studying individual galaxies allows.

ideas.title: Bayesian Hierarchical Framework for Testing and Propagating Calibration Drift Across Cosmic Time
ideas.description: This research direction develops a Bayesian hierarchical model that treats the parameters of local abundance diagnostics (e.g., line ratio calibrations, ionization correction factors) not as fixed truths, but as latent variables with priors based on local measurements. The model then simultaneously infers these calibration parameters and the astrophysical parameters (chemical abundances, evolutionary trends) from higher-redshift data. This allows for a rigorous, probabilistic test of calibration validity across cosmic time, where the posterior distributions reveal if and how calibrations drift. Crucially, it propagates the uncertainty from this potential drift directly into the final uncertainties on cosmic chemical evolution, producing more robust and interpretable constraints.

ideas.title: Adapting Hierarchical Bayesian Models from Epidemiology to Stellar Abundance Analysis
ideas.description: This idea adapts the solution of hierarchical Bayesian modeling from epidemiology to the problem of quantifying parameter uncertainties in stellar abundance analysis. In epidemiology, such models are crucial for integrating disparate, noisy data sources to estimate uncertain disease parameters and forecast trends. Analogously, in stellar astrophysics, we can construct a hierarchical Bayesian model that treats individual stellar abundances as data, with uncertainties, that inform higher-level parameters describing galactic chemical evolution. The adapted solution will systematically pool information across stars in different galactic regions, using priors from theoretical evolution models, to provide robust, probabilistically quantified uncertainties on derived abundances and evolutionary trends, transforming point estimates into full posterior distributions.

ideas.title: Adapt Medical PET-CT Fusion Imaging for 3D Chemical Mapping in ELGs
ideas.description: This idea adapts the solution to a parallel problem in medical diagnostic imaging—specifically, the fusion of functional PET scans with structural CT scans—to the astrophysical context of mapping chemical abundances in distant Emission Line Galaxies (ELGs). In medicine, software algorithms are used to co-register 3D metabolic activity data with high-resolution anatomical imagery, pinpointing functional hotspots within physical structures. The adapted research idea applies this data fusion paradigm by taking 3D integral field spectroscopy data (providing the 'functional' map of chemical abundances and kinematics) and computationally fusing it with deep, high-resolution broadband imaging (e.g., from HST or JWST, providing the 'structural' map of stellar morphology and dust lanes). The core methodological transfer involves developing and applying analogous image registration, spatial alignment, and pixel/voxel correlation techniques to create unified, precise 3D maps where chemical enrichment patterns are directly visualized within the detailed galactic anatomy.

ideas.title: Adapting Seismic Horizon Tracking for UV Disk Segmentation
ideas.description: This idea adapts a solution from geophysical seismic imaging, where algorithms are used to track faint, continuous subsurface geological horizons across noisy 2D sections or 3D volumes. The analogous solution uses methods like skeletonization and seed point propagation that connect low-contrast, coherent features based on local texture and structural continuity. Adapted to the astronomical context, these algorithms would be retrained or reconfigured to identify and segment faint UV disk structures by tracking luminosity gradients and coherent emission patterns in noisy UV data, propagating from user- or model-defined seed points to automatically outline the entire diffuse disk complex.

ideas.title: Physics-Informed Synthetic Data Generation and Domain Adaptation for Robust UV Disk Segmentation
ideas.description: This adversarially improved approach first generates a large, physically realistic synthetic dataset of UV disk structures and their corresponding ground-truth segmentations using radiative transfer models that incorporate known instrumental noise profiles. A deep segmentation network is pre-trained on this synthetic data to learn robust priors for disk morphology. The system then employs unsupervised domain adaptation, specifically feature-level alignment techniques, to bridge the gap between the clean synthetic data and noisy, artifact-laden real UV observations, enabling accurate segmentation without manual annotation of real data. This end-to-end pipeline is explicitly designed to overcome the fundamental domain shift that would cause direct transfer of medical imaging algorithms to fail.
